A cold front blew through from the north Friday evening with only a few light showers. Overnight, a stiff NW wind developed, driving cold air into the Southern Appalachians. Saturday is brisk—a sharp contrast to the last few days. After a frosty Sunday morning, temperatures moderate only modestly Sunday afternoon. Gradual warming begins Monday.

Late March Chill

Last Updated Sat March 28 at 10:54AM by Ray Russell

A cold front blew through from the north Friday evening with only a few light showers. Overnight, a stiff NW wind developed, driving cold air into the Southern Appalachians. Saturday is brisk—a sharp contrast to the last few days. After a frosty Sunday morning, temperatures moderate only modestly Sunday afternoon. Gradual warming begins Monday.

Forecast Discussion

A front moved through Friday evening with only a few light showers. The larger impact was gusty winds Friday night and colder temperatures.

High pressure is centered in the middle of the country today. It is centered over the Chesapeake Bay Sunday morning, then moves into the Atlantic Monday.

Saturday is chilly with a breezy morning. Tonight is relatively cold, with a frosty Sunday morning. Temperatures moderate somewhat Sunday afternoon.

As the center of high pressure moves east, our flow becomes southwesterly. As a result, gradually warmer weather arrives Monday and Tuesday. Both days are dry.

The next cold front approaches Wednesday and is near us Thursday and Friday. Details on its exact location are uncertain, but scattered showers develop Wednesday afternoon through Thursday. The front may briefly be to our south Thursday before returning as a warm front Friday. A few showers may linger into Friday. I kept the rain and temperature forecasts near the middle of the widely differing model guidance. Regardless of those differences, a warming trend begins next Saturday.

Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters.

An Explainer: Eastern & Western Escarpment
Mountains Make Weather

One of the most common questions we get on a weekly basis at Ray’s Weather is not a strictly weather-adjacent one! If you are a long-time reader of Ray’s, you have seen countless references to a few prominent geographic features of our region: the Western Escarpment, the Eastern Escarpment, and the Blue Ridge. These features are very important for many local weather phenomena, and often act as sharp dividing lines between different types of weather simultaneously.

You may well know what those things are, but if that isn’t the case for you, we are here to clear the air on what we mean when we reference those places. First up…

The Western Escarpment

Before we even get into the specifics of where this region lies, we have to define what an “escarpment” really is. Merriam-Webster offers: "A long cliff or steep slope separating two comparatively level or more gently sloping surfaces and resulting from erosion or faulting."

Quite simply, the escarpment is the dividing region between foothills and mountains. It is a broader area that encompasses where the foothills begin to rise more dramatically up to higher terrain.

The Western Escarpment region runs along the base of the Smoky Mountains, following the Tennessee line to Madison County, before things get a bit muddier between there and Ashe County. It generally hugs the Tennessee line in Yancey and Mitchell Counties to Roan Mountain, but then dips into North Carolina more, with Beech and Sugar Mountain lying just to the east of the Western Escarpment region. After that, it cuts north past Snake Mountain and Pond Mountain, before curving eastward around the Grayson Highlands.

The Eastern Escarpment

This one is a bit easier to see and define. The dividing line between mountain and foothill is more pronounced on the eastern slopes of the Southern Appalachians. The Eastern Escarpment parallels the Blue Ridge itself, running through far northern South Carolina, before cutting north through Chimney Rock, through McDowell County to Linville Gorge, staying east of Grandfather Mountain and The Blowing Rock, riding the county lines northeastward to the Virginia border. From there, it snakes up to Bent Mountain and Roanoke as it leaves the Ray’s Weather region.

Places like Lake James, Edgemont, and Stone Mountain State Park are within the Eastern Escarpment region. Not in the mountains, but right up against them.

The Blue Ridge

Lastly, the thin blue line that parallels the Eastern Escarpment is what we know as “The Blue Ridge.” This one may have some disagreement associated with it, as the entire mountain range in North Carolina is often referred to as “The Blue Ridge Mountains.” In our case, we are referring to a specific, semi-continuous ridgeline that marks the final, eastern frontier of the higher terrain, before dropping down into the escarpment region.

The easiest way to see what we mean is to take a ride on the Blue Ridge Parkway from just east of Mount Mitchell all the way up to Roanoke. The Parkway essentially rides right along the Blue Ridge in those areas. For example, if you are standing on Grandfather Mountain and looking east, you are along the crest of the Blue Ridge and gazing into the Eastern Escarpment region.

Why These Features Matter

Both escarpments and the Blue Ridge itself are very important dividing lines and focal points for several different genres of weather in the Southern Appalachians. The Western Escarpment is often referred to in the context of our snow events. When cold fronts blow down from the northwest, they send air rising up the higher terrain near the North Carolina/Tennessee border, producing the snow showers that favor west over east time and time again. It’s why Blowing Rock will never get as much snow as Beech Mountain in a northwest flow event: it isn’t just colder up top, there is also better access to air being forced up the mountains to produce precipitation.

Meanwhile, on the Eastern Escarpment, you often hear us use the words “wedge” or “cold-air damming” to describe situations where cold air works its way down the east side of the Appalachians and often delivers gray, misty conditions to the Foothills and eastern mountains. The Eastern Escarpment and Blue Ridge are both important for cold-air damming (or CAD) events, as the cold air layer is so shallow that it is unable to work over the Blue Ridge. Areas closer to the Western Escarpment can experience mild, sunny days while Hickory and Wilkesboro fail to leave the 30s and 40s under cloudy skies. Sometimes, you can find a spot along the Blue Ridge to view the fog and clouds beneath you out over the Foothills!

Fog over the Piedmont, viewed from western Polk County (Image: Evan Fisher)

The Blue Ridge can also act in a similar fashion to the Western Escarpment during periods of moist southerly or southeasterly flow. The terrain forces the air upwards, and more clouds and precipitation is produced in a region of local enhancement. This was the case to an extreme degree during Helene, as exceptionally moist air rushed up from the south and southeast, resulting in the incredible, devastating rainfall totals of up to 30+ inches that were observed during that storm. These are certainly not the only major weather patterns that are influenced by the local terrain, but they are some of the most commonly affiliated with the escarpments and Blue Ridge.

Lastly, we often refer to a variety of peaks in the Southern Appalachians. The map below gives you an excellent reference point for where your town lies in relation to the most notable peaks of our area.
